coredata.dc:description: Mpox, a zoonotic disease caused by the Mpox virus (MPXV), has re-emerged as a significant public health threat, particularly following the outbreak in 2022. Early and rapid detection of MPXV is crucial for controlling viral spread and preventing severe complications, particularly in vulnerable populations. While effective, traditional diagnostic methods like Polymerase Chain Reaction and genome sequencing are often costly and require complex equipment. Nanomaterial-based biosensors offer a promising alternative due to their unique physicochemical properties, high surface area, biocompatibility, and rapid response times. This review explores various nanomaterials—such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, quantum dots (QDs), and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)—and their application in the development of biosensors for MPXV detection, focusing on research from 2008 to 2024. These materials enable sensitive, specific, and portable biosensors that detect MPXV in real time via electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric, and calorimetric mechanisms. Each detection method leverages the virus's interaction with nanomaterial-functionalized surfaces to generate measurable signals. The review also discusses the advantages of nanomaterial-based biosensors, including enhanced sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and portability, alongside the current challenges and future directions in the field. Nanomaterial-based biosensors could play a vital role in public health efforts to manage Mpox outbreaks by enabling early detection in clinical and point-of-care settings.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: List of various nanoparticles for biosensor and Schematic diagram of these nanomaterials-based biosensors for Mpox detection.

Diagram illustrating the architecture of the portable CRISPR-SPR-FT biosensing system for MPXV identification. Partial complementary DNAs were used to adorn the suggested CRISPR-SPR-FT biosensor with AuNPs. The biosensor was then built to contain ssDNA reporters with important trans-cleavage sites. When Cas12a–crRNA is activated by target DNA, ssDNA reporters are trans-cleaved.The fiber-based biosensing device transmits and records the spectral signal in real time during CRISPR/Cas12a sensing. Changes in the SPR signal are caused by the loss of AuNPs on the sensor surface [39].

(A) Diagrammatic illustration of MPXV electrochemical detection using a portable, smartphone-controlled potentiostat coupled to a nano biosensor (LSG/AuNS/Ab-MPXV/BSA). (B) Plots for elevated A29 protein concentrations in 10.0 mmol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.4) at concentrations ranging from 1 × 10−14 to 1 × 10−7 g mL−1. (C) An analytical curve shows the relationship between the logarithm of the A29 protein concentration and normalized RCT values taken from Nyquist plots. (D) Nyquist charts for tittered MPXV viral strain at linear concentrations between 1 × 10−1 and 1 × 104 PFU mL−1. (E) Normalised RCT values taken from Nyquist plots and transformed into an analytical curve as a function of the logarithm of the MPXV viral loads. (F) According to these nano biosensors' analytical GREEness Metric method (AGREE), their process yields a scale of 0.83. In triplicate (n = 3), all EIS measurements were performed using 2.5 μL of A29 protein or MPXV samples and incubated on the biosensor surface for 10 min. The measurements were recorded with a frequency ranging from 1 × 105 to 0.1 Hz and an amplitude of 10 mV, while 5.0 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6] 3−/4− in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl [58].

The dual-signal ICA method's analytical performance. Comprehensive fluorescence signals on the T line, colorimetric and fluorescence images, and the matching calibration curve of test strips for MPXV antigen detection are shown in (a) and (c). (b) T-line SEM pictures from the (i) positive and (ii) negative samples. Results of the (d) AuNP-based ICA and (e) ELISA for the MPXV detection. (f) Confirmation that the suggested assay is reproducible. (g) MoS2-TQD-ICA detection performance in (i) actual saliva samples and (ii) actual lake water [73].

The epidemiological features, pathophysiology, clinical diagnosis, and therapy of Mpox. (A) The transmission of Mpox occurs by animal-to-animal, animal-to-human, and human-to-human channels. (B) Clinical signs often appear following Mpox infection. (C) Symptoms of Mpox infection may vary depending on the immunological state and clinical treatment options, and clinical treatment options are included [84].
illustrates the schematic and operations of the light-switch microfluidic sensor controlled by ECL for Mpox analysis [105].

A schematic showing PD-IRIS workflow for MPXV detection in a POC format. First, the sample (MPXV) is mixed with specific detection antibodies (A29 mAb), and then, this mixture flows over the PD-IRIS chip assembled in a microfluidic cartridge. As the antibody-decorated viruses are captured on the surface-spotted protein G, bound particles appear as white dots on the camera following an image processing step. The overall signal is calculated as particle density (particles/mm2), and the binding curves are generated to show the signal on protein G and negative (streptavidin) spots. The figure was made using the BioRender software [116].

Shows the integrated assay device's schematic design. 8 (A) The detecting device's overview. The disposable nucleic acid extraction module has a flip-top design 8A ①. Through the use of a card slot, the nucleic acid extraction module 8A ① may be linked to the integrated heating module 8A ② and visualization result readout module 8A ③. (B) The method and progress detection. (A) Drawing Samples. 30 μL of Si-OH MBs are present in the lysis well (⑿), and 1000 μL of 80 % ethanol (aq) are present in the washing well (⑤). The drying well (⑥) has a circular hollow form. The designs of the wells ⑿, ⑤, and ⑥ are all inverted cones. (b) The module for extracting nucleic acids. (c) The LAMP amplification module was supplied with mineral oil and reaction premix tubes for amplification. (d) A smartphone or the unaided eye can interpret the visual colorimetric data (yellow = positive, red = negative) (Video S1). (The reader is directed to the online version of this article for an explanation of the color references in this figure legend.). Fig. 8B illustrates these devices' parts and capabilities. Using a swab, microorganisms were extracted from the skin's surface and then agitated in a lysis buffer. The nucleic acid extraction module received the resultant mixture, which included MPXV DNA (Fig. 8B (a)). Second, the nucleic acid extraction module, which has three separate wells and a magnetic lid, was used. Viral lysis, washing, and air-drying were all handled by three different wells. To extract MPXV DNA, the lysis well was prefilled with Si-OH MBs and covered with a tin foil sealing sheet. On eliminate residues and inhibitors adsorbed on Si-OH MBs, 1000 μL of 80 % ethanol (aq) was preloaded into the washing well. The drying well's many apertures allowed for quick air drying. To concentrate Si-OH MBs adsorbed with nucleic acids to the bottom of the lid, a modified magnetic lid with a hollow center and sealed bottom that housed a tiny magnetic rod was put on the lysis well (④). After that, the whole assembly was moved to the washing well (⑤). Si-OH MBs were air-dried in the drying well (⑥). Thirdly, the heating module was inserted after the Si-OH MBs containing concentrated DNA were added to the LAMP reaction mixture. For 40 min, this reaction was carried out at 65°C (Fig. 8B (c)). Lastly, the colorimetric readout module (Fig. 8B (d)) allowed for the findings to be visually examined and compared to the no template control (NTC) tube [134–138].
Schematic representation of colorimetric sensors for pathogen identification based on functionalized AuNPs [139].

(A) The virus surface acoustic wave (SAW) biosensor concept based on antibodies. A biotinylated (B) monoclonal anti-JVB antibody against the Coxsackie virus was attached to the lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) sensor surface after it was coated with NeutrAvidin Biotin Binding Protein (N-Avidin). SNV detection was achieved by direct coupling of the anti-SNV-G1 glycoprotein scFv antibody. A shift in the input frequency of 325 MHz results from the molecular interaction between the virus and antibody. (B) Four aluminum delay lines are visible on the sensor wafer; one is the reference line, and the other three are the test delay lines. The sensor wafer is displayed in proportion to a dime coin. (C) This image shows the SAW detector board (thin arrow), fluidic housing (thick arrow), and output interface device (arrowhead) connected to a laptop computer [164].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mpox, a zoonotic disease caused by the Mpox virus (MPXV), has re-emerged as a significant public health threat, particularly following the outbreak in 2022. Early and rapid detection of MPXV is crucial for controlling viral spread and preventing severe complications, particularly in vulnerable populations. While effective, traditional diagnostic methods like Polymerase Chain Reaction and genome sequencing are often costly and require complex equipment. Nanomaterial-based biosensors offer a promising alternative due to their unique physicochemical properties, high surface area, biocompatibility, and rapid response times. This review explores various nanomaterials—such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, quantum dots (QDs), and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)—and their application in the development of biosensors for MPXV detection, focusing on research from 2008 to 2024. These materials enable sensitive, specific, and portable biosensors that detect MPXV in real time via electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric, and calorimetric mechanisms. Each detection method leverages the virus's interaction with nanomaterial-functionalized surfaces to generate measurable signals. The review also discusses the advantages of nanomaterial-based biosensors, including enhanced sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and portability, alongside the current challenges and future directions in the field. Nanomaterial-based biosensors could play a vital role in public health efforts to manage Mpox outbreaks by enabling early detection in clinical and point-of-care settings.

ce:section.ce:para: M-pox, derived from the term ‘Monkey Pox,’ is a rare disease caused by the M-pox virus. The first cases of M-pox were detected in the Democratic Republic of Congo in 1970, but the latest outbreaks took place in June 2022 [1]. WHO declared a “public health emergency of international concern” in 2022 because a virus belonging to clade IIb, another group of viruses, affected 60,000 people in just one month [2]. The most alarming part is the virus's adaptability, with different characteristics that result in death even in regions with no previous record of M-pox presence [3]. The patient comes with the symptoms of painful papules, vesicles, and pustules, but the incubation period may last for 21 days [4]. Globally, M-pox has four different clades in different regions, each with variations of characteristics. However, the most common primary symptom is rash, mainly in the genital and anorectal areas or in the mouth, which is different from other types of pox [5]. Though M-pox is a zoonotic disease, it can be transmitted from person to person by close, face-to-face, intimate, and sexual contact with an affected person. The unborn baby can be affected by this deadly virus [6]. This constant change in the virus's character makes implementing the required vaccine challenging [7]. The impact of M-pox disease is drastically high, where multiple types of individuals have higher possibilities for being affected. Pet raisers, health workers, sexual workers, travelers, and social workers are in the danger zone of this virus [8]. It is an international public health concern where the entire chain of each citizen is affected. The civilian health workers must be infected without the proper safety guards, and the medication and vaccination process will also be withstood [9]. This adaptability underscores the need for continuous research and vigilance in the fight against M-pox.

Early detection of the Monkeypox Virus (MPXV) is crucial; it's a matter of life and death. The heightened susceptibility of children, pregnant women, and individuals with compromised immune systems to severe illness and potentially fatal complications such as hemorrhagic fever underscores the urgency of this issue. The virus, with no defined metabolic structure, relies entirely on the host cell's energy. Early detection and treatment can halt the growth of MPXV virus cells, preventing the onset of symptoms such as fever, back pain, myalgia, headache, and skin diseases like rashes. Despite these symptoms, the surety of the presence of MPXV can be determined by laboratory tests after the initial symptoms appear [10–12]. On the other hand, the treatment after detection is essential, but the drug and vaccine have not been designed properly till now. Most designs are based on smallpox origin, which is not specified for the M-pox virus [13]. The vaccine and medicine need to be designed with specific variations of ages. Additionally, different clades with different regions have variations of diseases, where detection techniques and medication should be unconventional. In that case, vaccines need to be implemented after justifying all the cases that have not been adequately applied yet [14].

Biosensors, with their biological sensing elements, are not just tools in the fight against M-pox; they are potent weapons. These analytical devices leverage biology's sensitivity, specificity, and physicochemical transducers to provide comprehensive bioanalytical measurements in simple, user-friendly formats [15,16]. They offer a sensitive and accurate alternative to traditional methods such as culturing cells, detecting antigens or antibodies, performing hemagglutination assays, detecting nucleic acids, and sequencing genes [17]. Introducing nanoparticle-based sensors has further advanced this field, developing Nano-biosensors that provide even more precise results. With this knowledge and these tools at your disposal, you are not just a participant in the battle against M-pox but a vital player, empowered to make a significant impact.

Nanomaterial-based biosensors have the potential to detect M-pox Virus. Carbon Nanotubes, Graphene, Photonic crystals, and Quantum dots-based Nanoparticles are effective biosensors [18]. Nanoparticles enhance rapid and sensitive detection to identify MPXV earlier than other phenomena [19]. Carbon nanotubes have higher sensitivity and rapid responsibility to detect pathogenic viruses in advanced technology [20]. Gold- and graphene-based nano biosensors also have the same characteristics for Mpox treatment using rapid detection and diagnostics tools [21,22]. These biosensors can facilitate the early recognition of foreign particles, particularly viruses, thereby improving the identification of potential antigens [23]. Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR), Genome Sequencing, and Isothermal Amplification methods can help detect MPXV, but they are still costly and portable for equipment [24].

Electrochemical, optical, thermal, electronic, piezoelectric, and magnetoelastic mechanisms can be used for rapid detection [25], which assist in using nanomaterial-based sensors to complete targeted detection for a patient suffering from the M-pox virus. Despite that, some minor issues with nanoparticle-based sensors like Graphene require high stability and sensitivity to detect the problems [26]. Still, carbon-based nanoparticles show a higher possibility of fast detection than other nanoparticles [27]. It is undoubtedly essential to address Monkeypox early on to prevent the possibility of it developing into an epidemic similar to Covid-19.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of nanomaterials used in biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The small size, electronic properties, biocompatibility, and high surface area to volume ratio of nanostructured materials give them unique physical and chemical characteristics [28–31]. Investigating these unique characteristics improved biosensors' characteristics and increased their detection range. Biosensors have been developed throughout the last 25 years to supplement PCR and ELISA for pathogen identification. Biosensors offer supplementary platforms to PCR and ELISA for pathogen detection and quantification. They are based on directly integrating a sensitive transducer and selective bio-recognition elements without requiring sample preparation; biosensors have made it possible to detect infections in various contexts in real-time with high sensitivity and selectivity [32]. Electrochemical biosensors, optical biosensors and lateral flow biosensor have been extensively applied to MPox detection in multiple types of biosensors.

Carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphene), quantum dots, and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are examples of common nanostructured materials utilized in electrochemical and optical biosensors [33–35]. Types of these nanoparticles and the schematic diagram of these nanomaterials-based biosensors for Mpox virus detection are illustrate in Fig. 1 .

Due to their many advantages, including exceptional optical performance, unique catalytic activity, outstanding biocompatibility, and ease of regulated production, gold nanoparticles are currently regularly applied to optical biosensors [36,37]. The physical component used by these biosensors is light, which can be altered, absorbed, or released due to a chemical reaction between the analyte and the biorecognition layer. Because of their sensitivity and selectivity, these platforms are very intriguing for detecting bacterial pathogens [38]. Moreover, optical biosensors don't alter the characteristics of the target molecules and don't require laborious pretreatment. Surface-plasmon resonance (SPR) [39] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [40] are two types of optical biosensors used for MPXV detection. Early detection of MPXV infection is expected to benefit from these methods.

By detecting variations in the surface load, optical fiber SPR biosensors are excellent in immunodetection in liquid-phase conditions. A DNA aptamer-decorated fiber SPR biosensor for the precise measurement of inflammatory biomarkers was recently described by Hua et al. [41]. Its detection limit (LOD) for cardiac troponin I and C-reactive protein was 2.5 and 1.7 nM, respectively. Additionally, Zhang et al. [42] previously created an SPR-based fiber biosensor that can detect urine glucose samples using an enzymatic reaction carried out by a homemade glucose breakdown device, yielding a wide detection range of 0–400 mg/dL. Similarly, the trans-cleavage process of ssDNA reporters on an SPR fiber tip biosensor may generate surface load changes, proving the idea's practicality and the particular CRISPR/Cas12a method can identify target viral DNA. Therefore, a compact and portable SPR-based fiber tip biosensing device was integrated into the CRISPR/Cas12a-based diagnostic platform to overcome the shortcomings of the CRISPR-empowered SPR platform and accomplish the quick and portable detection of MPXV.

Fig. 2 . demonstrates the concept of the target MPXV DNA detection platform. They used two partially complementary DNAs with an ssDNA reporter to create a CRISPR-SPR-FT biosensor embellished with AuNPs. When MPXV DNA is present, the CRISPR/Cas12a system may trans-cleave ssDNA reporters to liberate the AuNPs, lowering the surface load on the sensor surface and, in turn, the SPR signal.

For precise MPXV detection, the CRISPR-SPR-FT biosensing platform offers the following main benefits: (1) high sensitivity for amplification-free sample detection (LOD: < 5 aM); (2) the capacity to identify the particular L108F mutation site in MPXV; and (3) portability, which satisfies the requirement for extensive point-of-care investigations. Overall, this CRISPR-powered biosensor makes accurate, consistent, sensitive, and trustworthy gene analysis of the MPXV possible [39].

There is another method, which is the loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) method. It has proven to be a practical and dependable approach for the detection and identification of microbial and viral pathogens [43], and in resource-constrained regions, such as poor nations and rural areas, lateral flow biosensors (LFBs) have demonstrated themselves to be quick, sensitive, and economical point-of-care and in-field diagnostic techniques [44]. Xiao F. et al. [45] introduced the MPXV-LAMP-LFB diagnostic test, which combines the LFB based on gold nanoparticles and LAMP to detect MPXV easily, sensitively, and precisely. This AuNPs-based lateral flow biosensor is used to detect the presence of two genetic clades of MPXV; the D14L gene is specific to MPXV in Central Africa, while the ATI gene is exclusive to MPXV in West Africa. Using commercially synthesized plasmids, pseudotyped viruses, and simulated clinical specimens, the MPXV-LAMP-LFB assay's sensitivity, specificity, and feasibility were assessed. According to the results, the newly created MPXV-LAMP-LFB assay is a potential diagnostic tool that could increase and speed up MPXV detection capabilities in endemic and non-endemic areas for point-of-care (POC) tests.

Graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are the principal components of graphene-based nanomaterials. A two-dimensional carbon substance known as graphene has a single atomic layer's thickness. Due to its distinct characteristics, graphene is extremely sensitive when identifying biological recognition components. It includes high electron mobility, mechanical qualities, chemical stability, thermal conductivity, and a large specific surface area [46,47]. Graphene was produced via Nano-biosensors by reducing graphene oxide [48]. Because of these unique characteristics, graphene has been thoroughly investigated in creating electrochemical biosensors. The detection of numerous biomolecules, including nucleic acids [49], glucose [50], cholesterol [51], proteins [52], dopamine [53], etc., has been accomplished using graphene-based electrochemical biosensors.

Within 35 min, MPXV may be immediately detected by the electrochemical biosensor, which targets various parts of the F3L gene and is based on rGO nanomaterials with Cas12a as nanocomposite [54], and exhibits antifouling solid activity in complicated biological fluids (such as whole blood and serum) with a detection limit of 682 fmol/L. PNA capture probes using the carbodiimide process were used to functionalize the nanocomposite biosensors. This rGO nanomaterials-based electrochemical biosensor enhanced Cas12a activation, collateral cleavage, and enhanced sensitivity for detecting the F3L gene of MPXV.

Laser-scribed graphene (LSG) devices are used for electrochemical biosensors and are extensively applied in MPXV detection. Through direct, single-step, maskless laser writing under environmental circumstances and with a scalable methodology, laser scribing transforms carbon-rich precursors into 3D graphene nanomaterial [55]. Because of its superior qualities, including a wide surface area, high conductivity, low cost, and ease of operation, LSG processing has drawn a lot of interest in the biosensor industry [56,57]. De Lima et al. [58] demonstrated the first electrochemical proof of concept for using a paper-based LSG nano-biosensor to detect MPXV antigen. They describe an electrochemical biosensor that uses readily available materials to identify MPXV antigens in saliva and plasma samples in less than 15 min. A monoclonal antibody and gold nanostructures (AuNS) were added to a paper substrate carved by a CO2 laser machine to create the electrochemical system, which allowed for the sensitive detection of the A29 viral protein. Based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), a smartphone-connected miniaturized potentiostat can perform the diagnostic test. Excellent analytical parameters provided by the impedimetric biosensing approach allowed for identifying A29 glycoprotein at concentrations between 1 × 10−14 and 1 × 10−7 g mL−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 × 10−16 g mL−1. Additionally, a detection limit of 7.8 × 10−3 PFU mL−1 made it possible to detect MPXV antigens at concentrations ranging from 1 × 10−1 to 1 × 104 PFU mL−1. All the findings of these electrochemical biosensors in MPXV detection are visual in Fig. 3 .

The term "buckytube" refers to carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which were initially described by Ssumio Ijima in 1991. These are hollow, cylindrical tubes with a fullerene hemisphere on top that are made of one, two, or more graphite layers. With a high surface area to volume ratio, these structures are highly stable, highly thermally conductive, and have minimal surface fouling. They also have excellent mechanical and electrical properties. They are stiff and strong because of the correct arrangement of carbon atoms connected by sp2 bonds [59]. The combination of nanowire shape, biocompatibility, and electrical characteristics greatly enhances CNT application in biosensing. The most often utilized nanomaterials in biosensors, diagnostics, and tissue engineering over the past few decades have been carbon nanotubes (CNTs). In 2006 [60], Tang and colleagues developed susceptible and responsive DNA sensors based on single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs). Semiconducting single-wall carbon nanotubes, or s-SWCNTs, were used by Li et al. [61] to create a biosensor that could detect dopamine at room temperature with a meager detection limit of 10−18 mol/Land a CNT-based biosensor with an excellent detection limit was created by Janssen et al. [62] to detect bovine serum albumin (BSA).

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconducting nanocrystalline solids that typically have a diameter of between 2.0 and 10.0 nm. They have already garnered a lot of attention for their innovative biosensing technology. Researchers use QDs extensively in sensing applications because of their remarkable optical, electrical, and size-dependent luminous characteristics [63–71]. It has been demonstrated that QDs provide exceptional charge carrier transport capabilities and a high surface-to-volume ratio, which may enhance a biosensor's functionality [72]. Because of its remarkable qualities, which include low toxicity, excellent solubility, chemical stability, and adaptability, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained attention in frontline sensing research projects [72,73].

Wang et al. [73] designed a two-dimensional film-like nanotag including a molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) core and multilayered QD nanoshells for dual-signal immunogen chromatography assay (ICA) to offer improved stability, a broader reaction interface, and superior signal responses compared to traditional spherical nano labels. Their suggested biosensor's specificity, accuracy, strength, and usefulness as a detector were also proven in clinical and environmental samples. For the quick, adaptable, and quantitative detection of the MPXV antigen in various contexts, they creatively suggested a colorimetric–fluorescence dual enhancement nanotag based on a monolayer MoS2 nanosheet. They incorporated it into a dual-signal output ICA platform. Since the target virus's surface envelope protein, A29, is highly conserved, it was chosen as the specific antigen for MPXV detection [73].

The dual-signal ICA method's analytical performance is shown in Fig. 4 . The colorimetric (i), fluorescence (ii), and identifying fluorescence signals on the T line of test strips for different MPXV A29 protein concentrations are shown in Fig. 4a. As the target viral antigen concentration dropped, the T line's black hue and red fluorescence signal strength decreased. Here, natural light and an excitation with 356 nm UV light exhibited black and red fluorescence. The T region of the blank control (0 ng/mL) showed no discernible colorimetric or fluorescence signals. SEM scans of the T lines showed that many film-like MoS2-TQDs were detected in the positive test (10 ng/mL), but the blank control test showed no nanotags (Fig. 4b). This suggests that the development of MoS2-TQD-virus immunocomplexes was the source of dual solid signals. The MPXV protein concentration dropped to 0.1 ng/mL, but the black band on the T line remained visible to the unaided eye (Fig. 4a i). This result confirmed the visual limit of detection (vLOD) of the MPXV antigen identified by the MoS2-TQD-ICA colorimetric mode was confirmed to be 0.1 ng/mL. In the meantime, the fluorescence signal on the T line for the target antigen was shown to have a visual sensitivity of 0.01 ng/mL when exposed to UV illumination. Next, we utilized our portable dual-mode reader to thoroughly examine the test strips' fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4a ii), and we also used it to create the matching calibration curve for MPXV (Fig. 4c). With a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998, the resulting sigmoidal calibration curve showed that the MPXV antigen has a broad dynamic range of 100–0.005 ng/mL. The suggested ICA method's repeatability was investigated, as seen in Fig. 4f. as evidence of this approach's excellent stability and dependability, the RSD of the fluorescence intensity on the T line for the three MPXV groups (5, 0.1, and 0.005 ng/mL) was less than 8.75 %.

Viral binding events between the virus and biorecognition regions on the nanomaterial enable the detection of the M-pox virus using nanomaterial-based biosensors. This binding causes the nanomaterial's chemical or physical characteristics to change. These changes are then converted into signals that can be measured, enabling quick and accurate viral identification [74]. For early diagnosis and successful public health responses, the combination of sensitivity, speed, specificity, and portability provided by using nanomaterial-based biosensors for M-pox virus detection is precious. Due to these benefits, they can be widely implemented in clinical and field settings, helping to improve the treatment and containment of viral outbreaks [75]. There are two kinds of pox infections: those caused by the variola major virus and those caused by the variola minor virus, often known as the alastrim virus. While infections with the variola major virus were associated with severe disease and a high fatality rate (20–50 %), infections with the variola minor virus caused mild sickness. They had a low death rate of around 1 %. Aerosol or droplet contact is how the human smallpox virus spreads [76]. The Mpox virus naturally inhabits some rodents and monkeys in central Africa. Early human infections are usually associated with interaction with infected animals, such as eating undercooked meat or touching bodily fluids, tissues, or mucous membranes. Additionally, scratches or bites from infected animals may spread the infection [77]. It is thought that direct contact with respiratory droplets from infected people is how human-to-human transmission happens [78]. Moreover, the Mpox virus may spread vertically from moms who are sick to their infants. Fig. 5
, unlike earlier outbreaks, this new Mpox infection outbreak was the greatest documented pandemic outside of Africa. Previously, a person's illness with Mumps could only be identified after coming into touch with infected animals or visiting areas where the disease was prevalent [79]. Nonetheless, the majority of Mpox cases in the present outbreak are linked to interpersonal sexual contact rather than travel or contact with infected animals [80]. Over the previous two years, the bulk of recorded instances of Mpox outbreaks have involved gay or bisexual men. According to a study, 41 % of gay or bisexual men who had HIV co-infected accounted for 98 % of cases among these individuals. Additionally, 73 % of the detected lesions were in the anal and genital areas [81]. The symptoms of Mpox extend from 14 to 21 days, while the incubation period is from 7 to 14 days [82]. The lengthy incubation period creates considerable hurdles for correct diagnosis, possibly leading to delayed medical treatment, illness development, and continued transmission of the virus [83].

Converting the biological contact between the virus and a recognition element into an electrical signal is a crucial step in the electrochemical mechanism of a nanomaterial-based biosensor used to detect the M-pox virus [85]. Because of their superior electrical conductivity, large surface area, and ease of functionalization, nanomaterials, including graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and conducting polymers, are selected [86]. The local environment at the sensor surface may alter due to the virus attaching itself to the functionalized nanomaterial. This could be a shift in the charge distribution, a surface potential modification, or an adjustment to the redox reactions [87]. Resistance to current flow at the sensor interface is known as the impedance, which the binding event can change. For instance, the binding of the virus increases the impedance in an EIS setup, which is detected as a signal [88]. When a virus binds to an amperometric sensor, an electrochemical reaction frequently mediated by the virus or a mediator might change the current [89]. Redox-active labels are occasionally employed to boost the signal. Even at low virus concentrations, these labels can produce a more potent electrochemical reaction during the binding event [90]. The biorecognition elements could be connected to enzymes such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The enzyme catalyzes a redox process, resulting in a detectable current or voltage when it binds to the virus [91].The processed data shows the presence and concentration of the Mpox virus in the sample, usually in real-time on a linked device or computer [92]. The electrochemical method is appropriate for early diagnosis since it can identify the M-pox virus in extremely low quantities [93]. Electrochemical sensors' quick results often in only a few minutes are essential for prompt intervention. Clinical settings can employ electrochemical nanomaterial-based biosensors for the early detection and surveillance of the M-pox virus [94]. In this configuration, antibodies that are specific to the proteins of the Mpox virus functionalize graphene. There is a discernible shift in the material's conductance as a result of the virus attaching to these antibodies, which alters the charge distribution on the graphene. This shift is quantified as a change in current, which has a direct correlation with the viral load in the sample [95]. The first report of the human Mpox virus was published in 1970, and an epidemic of cases has been recorded lately [96]. To far, >90,000 Mpox-positive cases and 156 associated fatalities have been documented in 114 countries. Although the number of new positive cases has fallen gradually, real-time surveillance is vital to restrict the spread of Mpox until its elimination, notably in Africa and Asia, where the prevalence of infection is still on the increase. As a basis for efficient disease monitoring, accurate and prompt analysis of the virus is vital in minimizing the incidence of misdiagnosis or omissions and assuring precise disease prevention and control [97]. The presently available methods for detecting Mpox include polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and derivative technologies, which have attained exceptional sensitivity and specificity [98]. Furthermore, the absence of competent testing facilities slows the response to disease transmission and negatively affects public health planning. Therefore, a faster and easier approach for Mpox testing is necessary. Recently, the fast development of new-generation biosensors has led to the creation of smaller portable detection devices with better performance [99]. In most testing procedures, nucleic acid amplification is needed to guarantee sensitivity. Compared with PCR assay, which generally needs very sophisticated and costly apparatus to achieve cyclic thermal control, isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods are more appropriate for use in point-of-care (POC) testing. Recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) is an isothermal amplification approach that is started by a primer–recombinase complex that provides stable and efficient analyte amplification without a denaturation step within 20 min [100]. Moreover, RPA is easier than other isothermal amplification techniques, including as loop-mediated isothermal amplification, which needs six primers and reaction durations of > 60 min [101]. The electrically triggered luminescence reaction known as electrochemiluminescence (ECL), which may be repeated on an electrode surface, provides a useful method for creating sensors with a high signal-to-noise ratio and sensitivity, ease of downsizing, and wide range of detection [102]. In order to provide an amplification-free examination of ZIKV utilizing a drop of blood, Huang et al. described a dendritic Ru (bpy)2+/3 -polymer-amplified ECL diagnostic approach [103]. Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats technology was used with ECL sensors by Xie et al. to diagnose SARS-CoV-2 [104]. Nonetheless, the detecting probe's design in this investigation is intricate. Moreover, the detection probe's ability to hybridize with RPA products is limited by the double-stranded (ds) complementary DNA structure, making it unable to utilize directly to detect RPA products. We created a light-switch molecule-based ECL microfluidic detection chip with features including sample loading, RPA, and ECL detection for the sample-to-answer Mpox diagnosis in order to solve this problem. The reagent mixture for amplification and detection was preloaded into the microfluidic chip and isolated via the organic phase prior to usage, as seen in Fig. 6 . The sample loading region was sealed with a 3 M membrane to stop liquid evaporation during RPA. Following RPA, the products were centrifuged and combined with the ECL reaction mixture. This caused the light-switch molecule [Ru (phen)2dppz] 2+ to intercalate into dsDNA products, producing ECL emission that was triggered by an external voltage. To make sure that the quantitative analysis results of the target DNA could be evaluated in accordance with the light signal's intensity, the optical signal was gathered and amplified using a photomultiplier. Without the need for a specialized operation, the detection procedure can be finished in 20 min when combined with a hand-held detecting instrument. Utilizing 60 clinical samples from patients who tested positive for Mpox, including pustules, throats, urine, blood, saliva, and anus, we assessed the assay's performance. An LOD of 10 copies/μL and a sensitivity of 100 % were demonstrated by the Mpox test. This platform satisfies the stringent clinical requirements and is quick, simple to use, and affordable. As such, it has tremendous promise for the early detection of Mpox patients in POC testing [105].

On the other hand, the fact that electrochemical biosensors are sensitive, integrated, deployable in the field, and very simple to digitize suggests that they could be used to create innovative MPXV diagnostic systems [106]. The majority of these detection techniques, however, depend on target pre-amplification, which necessitates sophisticated equipment and adds to the assay's expense and duration. One promising substitute is amplification-free detection. This has led to the exploration of a number of approaches for the development of amplification-free MPXV detection, such as the combination of multiple crRNAs, aptasensor-based detection, electrochemical impedance-based mpox antigen detection, graphene quantum rods-based antigen sensing, detection based on nanopore sensing strategy, etc [107].

Graphene oxide, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and quantum dots (QDs) are common nanomaterials utilized in optical biosensors. These substances have distinct optical characteristics that can be used to identify viruses, such as plasmonic resonance, fluorescence, or absorbance [108]. The sensor is exposed to a biological sample (such as blood, saliva, or a swab) that contains the Mpox virus. The surface of the functionalized nanomaterial is where the virus particles or biomarkers disperse [109]. Surface plasmon resonance is a phenomena that gold nanoparticles show. It is caused by incoming light and results in collective oscillation of electrons on the nanoparticle surface. The SPR wavelength shifts as a result of the virus's surface binding, which modifies the local refractive index. An optical sensor detects this shift, which is correlated with the quantity of virus present [110]. The optical detection system of the sensor gathers and processes the optical signal, such as a shift in SPR, a change in fluorescence, or a change in color. The amount of M-pox virus in the sample is ascertained by analyzing the signal and comparing it to a calibration curve. The optical signal's wavelength or intensity is exactly proportional to the quantity of virus attached to the sensor [111]. Optical techniques frequently enable continuous surveillance by enabling real-time viral monitoring without harming the sample [112]. Semiconductor nanocrystals known as quantum dots glow when exposed to light. It is possible to combine antibodies specific to M-pox virus antigens with QDs in a biosensor. The antibody-functionalized QDs' fluorescence is either increased or quenched when the virus attaches to them. With the use of a fluorescence spectrometer, the change in fluorescence is monitored, and the signal intensity relates to the amount of virus present in the sample [113]. For early diagnosis and therapy, optical sensors are essential because they can identify the M-pox virus in its early stages, even at low viral levels [114]. Nanomaterial-based biosensors are new because of their increased sensitivity, quick reaction time, specificity, mobility, and digital technology integration for M-pox virus detection. With these advances, virus epidemics can be managed more effectively and public health outcomes can be improved. These discoveries represent major advancements over previous diagnostic approaches [115] (Fig. 7 ).

The ease of use, affordability, and immediate detection capabilities of optical sensors have made them a popular choice for viral detection. When it comes to viral diagnostics, an optical biosensor may identify antigens, entire viruses, viral nucleic acids, or antibodies made in reaction to the infection. Fluorescence-based optical sensors, surface plasmon resonance (SPR), optical resonators, and interferometry-based techniques are a few types of these sensors. Viral target molecules are caught on the surface by a virus-specific capture probe, and fluorescence-based sensors use fluorophore-attached detection molecules to bind to the molecules. While lab-based tests like ELISA need complex processes and limited sensitivity, fluorescence-based methods provide multiplexed detection. However, their performance is hindered by non-specific binding, photo bleaching, and poor sensitivity [117,118].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection determination for MPXV detection with PD-IRIS
ce:section.ce:para: The test procedure used is homogeneous in order to maximize the chip surface's capture efficiency. A molecule that has a high affinity for the antibody is spotted on the chip surface of a homogeneous test, in which the virions are statically treated with their matching antibody outside the system's fluidic channel (i.e., in an Eppendorf tube). Virions coated with antibodies are then flow-incubated across the spotted chip. During our investigations, we statically incubate several MPXV dilutions with the anti-A29 mAb. To capture flowing antibody-virus complexes, we mark protein G on the chip surface [119–121]. Three distinct viral samples, each containing 2.5 ng/mL of anti-A29 mAb, are generated at 5 × 104 PFU/mL, 1 × 104 PFU/mL, and 1 × 103 PFU/mL for the limit-of-detection (LOD) studies. The stock mAb solution (2.228 mg/mL) is diluted to 0.01 µg/mL in 1xPBS and filtered through a 0.02 µm Whatman Anotop filter in order to create homogenous virus-mAb combinations. 1xPBS solution is used to dilute the stock MPXV sample (4.6 × 106 PFU/mL) to 1 × 105 PFU/mL, 2 × 104 PFU/mL, and 2 × 103 PFU/mL. To create a homogeneous 100 µL sample for the experiment, combine 50 µL of diluted MPXV sample with 25 µL of diluted antibody solution and 25 µL of filtered 1x PBS. After that, the produced sample is incubated at room temperature for five minutes [122]. There are three phases in the flow incubation procedure. First, as a first wash step, filtered 1x PBS solution is flowing twice over the protein G spotted chip at 1500 µL/min for 20 sec per wash. After that, a homogeneous sample is used in lieu of the waste, and the syringe draws and pushes the virus solution over the chip three times in a row at a rate of 10 µL per minute, which equals 65 min of flow incubation. The chip is twice washed at the same speed after the incubation to get rid of non-specific binds. The camera continuously captures spot pictures at a rate of 16 frames per second while the homogeneous sample is in the flow incubation. One data point is created in a real-time binding curve by averaging the obtained photos every 30 sec. The surface-bound particles are counted for both the protein G spots and negative spots after the approximately one-hour incubation and washing. The bound particle density is expressed as particles/mm2 by dividing the counted particles by the spot area [123]. The HSV-1 homogeneous test, which uses the identical procedures as the MPXV assay to mix the HSV-1 sample with anti-A29 mAb and flow it over the PD-IRIS chip, yields the detection threshold. The detection threshold is derived as the mean particle density on the protein G spots (n = 6) plus three standard deviations. The concentration value at the point where the line hits the threshold signal line is found by linearly extrapolating the two lowest concentration data points to calculate the LOD for MPXV detection [124].

Bio receptors, which are substances such as antibodies, aptamers, or nucleic acids that selectively attach to the M-pox virus or its genetic components, are used to functionalize these nanomaterials [125]. The biosensor is exposed to the biological sample (such as blood, saliva, or swab extract) that may contain the M-pox virus. The surface of the functionalized nanomaterial is in touch with the sample [126]. The nanomaterials' bio receptors identify and attach to particular antigens on the surface of the M-pox virus or its genetic material, such as viral DNA/RNA. The biosensor is able to differentiate the M-pox virus from other viruses or biomolecules present in the sample [127]. A biological response, frequently comprising an enzyme process or a chemical reaction that is aided by the nanomaterials. An enzyme-linked reaction, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, or ELISA, may be used by the sensor to catalyze a process that produces heat [128]. The purpose of the calorimetric sensor is to pick up on even the smallest temperature variations brought on by the biological reaction [129]. The sensor monitors the heat production during the reaction, which is directly related to the virus load [130]. Usually, a thermocouple, thermistor, or other sensor-integrated temperature-sensitive materials are used to perform this [131]. The heat change is detected by the sensor, which then transforms it into an electrical signal. To determine the amount of virus in the sample, the signal is examined [132]. This system uses the sensitivity of calorimetric detection in conjunction with the specificity of molecular recognition to quickly and reliably detect the presence of the M-pox virus [133].

The three primary elements of the integrated sample-to-answer system are the colorimetric readout, LAMP reaction, and nucleic acid extraction Fig. 8A. The detachable module for nucleic acid extraction is disposable, and the heating module Fig. 8A ② and the visualization result readout module Fig. 8A ③ are merged into a single entity, capable of be recyclability. The device's small design makes it easier to use for testing at home or on the go.

Fig. 9 focuses on recent advances in the development of colorimetric sensors based on the unique physical properties of AuNPs for pathogen detection. It introduces strategies for colorimetric detection of pathogens in biological samples, including indirect target-mediated aggregation, the chromogenic substrate-mediated catalytic activity method, point-of-care testing (POCT) devices, and machine-learning-assisted colorimetric sensor arrays [139].

The M-pox virus is detected by a piezoelectric sensor nanomaterial-based biosensor that uses piezoelectric materials to translate mechanical changes brought on by virus binding into an electrical signal [140]. The primary sensing components are piezoelectric nanomaterials, such as quartz crystals, zinc oxide nanowires, or other piezoelectric polymers. The M-pox virus's antigens or genetic material are precisely recognized and bound to by the bio receptors on the nanomaterials' surface. Because of its excellent specificity, the sensor may easily distinguish the M-pox virus from other possible contaminants or viruses in the sample [141]. The piezoelectric nanomaterial experiences a mechanical deformation or stress due to this mass change [142]. The piezoelectric effect causes an electrical charge to be produced when the piezoelectric material undergoes mechanical deformation, such as bending, stretching, or compression. When mechanical stress is applied to a piezoelectric material, the substance experiences an electric potential known as the piezoelectric effect [143]. The label-free detection mechanism used by the piezoelectric sensor means that it can identify the presence of the virus without the need for additional labels, such as fluorescent or radioactive markers [144]. With the advancement of nanofabrication techniques, it is now possible to fabricate piezoelectric nanomaterial-based sensors in a scalable and economical manner. This opens the technology to broader application, especially in low-resource environments with problematic M-pox and other infectious diseases [145].

Table 1 describes the possible operation of a piezoelectric biosensor to identify the Mpox virus in a practical setting. Weather-related variables like humidity and temperature may influence the effectiveness of detection. Thus, tracking such information with detection data may be helpful.

Several anisotropic materials produce acoustic waves, translating electrical impulses into mechanical motion and vice versa. These substances are referred to as "piezoelectric," an effect that Pierre and Paul-Jacques Curie initially explained in the late 1800s [156]. Using acoustic waves to detect changes in mass or mechanical properties on the sensor surface when the virus binds to it is the process by which a surface acoustic wave (SAW) sensor nanomaterial-based biosensor for detecting the M-pox virus operates [157]. Surface acoustic waves are produced and received by interdigitated transducers (IDTs) on the surface of the SAW sensor. Upon application of an electrical signal, the IDTs produce mechanical acoustic waves that move along the sensor's surface [158]. The output IDTs receive the modified acoustic waves and transform the mechanical wave changes into an electrical signal. The quantity of the virus attached to the sensor surface is directly correlated with variations in wave amplitude or velocity [159]. The modified surface acoustic waves produce an electrical signal that is processed and examined. The amount and concentration of the M-pox virus in the sample are measured using the degree of change in the signal [160]. Since surface acoustic waves are sensitive to surface variations, nanomaterials work together to produce this effect—the possibility of downsizing would enable point-of-care diagnostics [161]. The extraordinary sensitivity of SAW biosensors to surface disturbances is well recognized. Their unique feature is their capacity to identify even minute mass alterations, which is perfect for detecting viral particles or the presence of viral antigens such as those found in the Mpox virus. When a target biomolecule, such as an antibody or DNA sequence, attaches to a particular receptor on the sensor surface, SAW sensors may detect changes in mechanical characteristics. There is a detectable change in the acoustic wave caused by the binding event [162]. Although a direct real-world example of a SAW biosensor that can detect Mpox specifically may not yet exist, this framework might be used to design such devices based on the proven principles of SAW technology in viral detection. The same technique also shows promise for Mpox detection, having been successfully used to identify Zika, influenza, and even SARS-CoV-2 [163] (Fig. 10 ).

Previously mentioned a "close relative" of the smallpox virus, pox is a zoonotic illness caused by an infection with the pox virus (MPXV), which is a member of the orthopoxvirus genus in the pox virus family [165,166]. Human pox incidences have significantly increased in several places since May 2022. Sore throat, fever, and rash are common symptoms of Mpox. However, other viruses, such as the varicella-zoster virus (VZV), herpes simplex virus types 1 (HSV-1), types 2 (HSV-2), and the B virus (BV), can also infect people and cause identical symptoms [167]. Since viruses can actuate deadly viral maladies, infections pose a genuine danger to open well-being. Disgraceful location instruments are the cause of these diseases' tall predominance. The requirement for a fast, exact, and infection-distinguishing-proof strategy is exceptionally high. Several biosensors have been created and brought to showcase and distinguish hurtful infections. A comparison of a few of the foremost broadly utilized infection location techniques' benefits and disadvantages can be seen in Table 2 .

A comparison of a few of the foremost broadly utilized infection techniques' benefits and disadvantages can be seen in Table 2. To distinguish pathogenic pathogens, we subsequently require speedy, simple, delicate, and exact methods [173]. Therefore, there has recently been an increasing interest in using biosensors in biomedical applications to identify alternative procedures without restrictions. This interest stems from the benefits of these technologies, which include fast-speed findings, outstanding sensitivity, and adequate selectivity. Furthermore, the need to precisely identify bacterial and viral illnesses using innovative techniques is rising because they threaten human life [174]. Biosensors are profoundly exact, delicate, and estimation frameworks that can decide exceptionally moo analyte concentrations in natural tests.

It is imperative to build a technology that can detect or feel the difficulty causing organic/inorganic biomolecules in a live creature since early detection of diseases can lead to better treatment outcomes and survival rates [175]. Because of its numerous preferences over conventional biosensors, the application of nanomaterial-based biosensors extends over various areas, such as nourishment security, natural checking, and therapeutic diagnostics (Fig. 11 ).

Unlike bulk materials, nanomaterials have extraordinary optical, electrical, chemical, and physical characteristics [176]. They act on the same scale as organic forms and may be promptly functionalized with intrigued substrates, making them exceptionally promising for biosensor improvements. Being one of the finest materials to form biosensors in such scourges, they are too highly reasonable due to their real-time discoveries [177]. A CRISPR/Cas12b-gFET biosensor allows amplification-free detection of mpox DNA at a sensitivity of approximately aM within 20 min, highlighting the potential of graphene in rapid diagnostics [178]. Antifouling nanocomposite electrochemical biosensors utilize numerous crRNA for point-of-care testing, improving affectability in complex organic liquids [179]. A lateral flow biosensor based on nanoparticles and the Multiple Cross Displacement Amplification (MCDA) approach allow for the rapid and susceptible detection of mPox in as little as five copies of the target gene. This detection may be accomplished in around 50 min [180].

The colorimetric characteristics of NMs, like AgNPs and Qdots, enable a favorable target to contact to be recognized by a color change; also, other NMs, like AuNPs, can be used. This color move is because gold nanoparticles have tall molar termination coefficient localized surface plasmon reverberation (LSPR) characteristics, which empower them to appear in size-dependent color shifts unmistakable to the unaided eye [181,182]. Recently, a nanocomposite AuNS@4 mercaptobenzoic acid @Au was formed using a modified structure of gold called "nanostars" (NSs), to which anchored DNA labeled with B-Chol formed a SERS nano shell [183]. The interactions between exosomes and the nanocomposite occurred by forming an immunocomplex that produced enhanced SERS signals. The detection limit was calculated to be as low as 27 particles per μL [184]. One biosensor that offers excellent sensitivity is affinity-based nano biosensors, which connect with receptors like ssDNA, antibodies, and aptamers to interact with nanoparticles [185].

The amazingly sensitive, exact, and focused estimation gadgets known as biosensors can identify low analyte concentrations in natural materials. The current nanotechnology center is evacuating the downsides of infection discovery methods to decrease costs and labor prerequisites. Biosensors have become more delicate and productive because of the consolidation of nanomaterials into their plan [186]. It has been illustrated that, including nanomaterials in miRNA, biosensors move forward with location execution and bring down the fetch of distinguishing low-abundance biomarkers [187]. Compared to conventional approaches, fluorescence polarization biosensors that include nanomaterials exhibit enhanced photostability and signal amplification, resulting in faster and more economical studies [188].

Portable and/or intelligent biosensor devices may represent a novel class of self-awareness and competency detectors, vital components of future intellectual gadgets. The dynamic intelligence lowered to the constituent equal with smart sensor devices may significantly impact claims in environmental safety, health monitoring, and medical diagnostics locally and globally [189,190]. Generating commercial biosensors based on nanomaterials for the location of microscopic organisms has not kept up with the headways in inquiry. Point-of-care testing and persistent observation can be made simpler by the integration of nanomaterial-based biosensors into wearable or convenient hardware [191]. Compact biosensors that are versatile and valuable in various settings, counting field and clinical settings, are made conceivable by nanomaterials. Using nanostructured materials to create shrewd sensors makes it feasible to form convenient gadgets with various detecting capabilities [192].

Various detection techniques each have benefits and Challenges. The best detection results are ensured in real-world applications by choosing the optimal detection technique depending on the particular needs of the situation. There are still several obstacles to MPXV detection As a pathogen with a biosensor, MPXV requires biosafety precautions to be taken during detection. To further avoid any unintentional infection transmission throughout the procedure, sample pretreatment needs to be carefully taken into account along with the biosensor for detection techniques employed. By using an inactivation technique that eliminates up to five log levels of infectious virus particles from viremic serum without sacrificing antibody detection, MPXV serology can be carried out outside of the BSL-3 facility [193].The epidemic features of MPXV are evolving along with the virus, with adolescents under the age of 15 emerging as a vulnerable demographic. Additionally, the MPXV is likely to co-infect with syphilis and HIV [194], which raises patient mortality, worsens prognosis, and causes a more severe illness. The differential diagnosis of mpox can also be made more difficult by conditions like chickenpox, shingles, herpes simplex, measles, dengue fever, and other febrile exanthemous disorders that exhibit similar clinical signs. Consequently, creating a variety of detection techniques is crucial for quick and effective differential diagnosis. Moreover some other challenges like Economic Challenges, lacks of bandgap, Robustness and Reproducibility, low Scalability and Production, etc. have to face during nanoparticle based biosensor use in Mpox detection.

Though nanoparticle-based biosensors offer low-cost detection procedures for MPox detection, some nanoparticles like AuNPs, are very expensive and it is a barrier in the medical and biosensor industries [195]. Also, the use of materials like indium in biosensor channels is costly, as are fabrication techniques, limiting their market viability [196]. Moreover, AuNPs aggregation in the presence of salt and the aptamer/analyte interaction allowed the graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets to regain their fluorescence upon the addition of the analyte [197]. The optical properties of AuNP suspension are altered by their aggregation, which causes a rise in size during synthesis and a corresponding change in diameter. This results in changes to the adsorption of light, which are accompanied by color variations [198]. This my create complexity in the biosensors for Mpox detectation.

Another NPs graphene, while premium graphene is a great conductor in biosensor, but it lacks a bandgap; consequently, when using graphene to create electronic microdevices, a bandgap has to be added. Making graphene and synthesizing it is a major challenge [199]. Another difficult problem is to create a bio-functionalization technique that can preserve high stability without altering the structure and functionality of proteins and graphene materials [200]. One of the difficulties is increasing the manufacturing of high-quality graphene, which is still a significant problem that will always affect the final characteristics of the materials [201] and developing scalable, economical production techniques for high-quality graphene and other nanomaterials remains difficult. The need for synthesis methods that ensure homogeneity and purity is emphasized across the graphene synthesis literature. With all of these, nanomaterial-based biosensors face another difficulty in durability and consistent reproducibility. Complex nanomaterial structures enhance detection but can reduce robustness and create manufacturing challenges in biosensor production.

The precise identification of the virus is essential to the diagnosis and management of MPox. For MPXV to be diagnosed, treated, prevented, and controlled, precise detection is essential. Using bio sensing technologies can increase the virus's sensitivity of detection, making it easier to identify infections at low levels all the way up to the acute phase. Even though POC biosensors are readily accessible, more technological advancements are needed to take the field to new heights. The assay time has been shortened by including IgG/IgM test strips in contemporary commercial diagnostic kits; nevertheless, their specificity and sensitivity still need to be improved [202]. Sensitivity enhancement techniques, such as fluidic control, enzyme-based signal improvement, and sample concentration, should be incorporated into the biosensors to improve detection sensitivity. Multiplexing ability is just as important as detection sensitivity to boost assay productivity. Specifically, the combination of nucleic acid testing with IgG/IgM testing would enable the detection of virus infection in both early and late phases, producing more accurate and consistent results [203].

Polymer-based electrochemical biosensors have attracted more attention recently than other biosensors because of their high sensitivity, affordability, and simplicity of manufacture [204]. Researchers are looking into more environmentally friendly ways to immobilize biomolecules on biosensors, which can be useful for the detection of mpox [205].

Researchers have recently tested the application of nanoparticles of metal, nanowires, nanopore arrays, and other metal micro-nano buildings to SPR biosensors in an effort to increase the responsiveness of these sensors by strengthening the nearby electric field through the coupling within confined SPR (LSPR) and SPR [206]. These biosensors exhibit a noticeable increase in sensitivity and exhibit high surface plasmon resonance (SPR) excitation to analytes. Based on these findings, we anticipate that in the near future, these biosensors may find use in chemical analysis, biological detection, and medical diagnostics [207]. Biosensing technology is at the cutting edge of clinical research due to the desire for new diagnostic instruments that are selective, cost-effective, portable, multipurpose, and sensitive. A viable and dependable platform for clinical analysis could be provided by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensors, which could offer label-free, sensitive, actual time tracking of biomolecular interactions [208]. Therefore, for greater sensitivity & detection constraints in biosensors, it is more important to improve the conventional enzyme-free amplification technique [209].

And these all will enhance to development of biosensors for Mpox detectation. With better analysis efficiency capable of matching or surpassing existing MPXV immunosensing methods, the GQRs-based composites platform may be a valuable future POC testing instrument for the prompt detection of MPXV infection. Future developments include putting composite materials on printed circuit boards and integrating a handheld biosensor for device manufacturing. Subsequent investigations need to concentrate on bolstering the resilience, mobility, and cost-effectiveness associated with these biosensors and examining innovative nanostructures that may augment their detection capacities.

The review on nanomaterial-based biosensors for early detection of the Mpox virus highlights the promise of these advanced diagnostic tools in transforming viral diagnostics. Major findings indicate that nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes, graphene, gold nanoparticles, and quantum dots are particularly effective in biosensor development due to their high surface area, biocompatibility, and enhanced signal transduction capabilities. These unique characteristics allow for highly sensitive, specific, and portable biosensors capable of real-time detection through mechanisms including electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric, and calorimetric responses. Such features position nanomaterial-based biosensors as a superior alternative to traditional diagnostics like PCR and ELISA, which, while accurate, are often costly, time-consuming, and impractical for POC use in low-resource settings.

However, current challenges must be addressed to fully harness these biosensors’ potential. Issues like production scalability, stability under varied environmental conditions, and consistent clinical validation limit broader adoption. Additionally, some nanomaterials exhibit limitations in real-world applications due to sensitivity loss over time, especially under challenging conditions such as high humidity or fluctuating temperatures. Overcoming these technical and environmental challenges through continued research on robust, cost-effective fabrication methods and enhanced biorecognition molecules could further improve biosensor performance.

Emerging opportunities in the field include the integration of biosensors with IoT and mobile health platforms, enhancing data accessibility, and allowing for continuous monitoring at POC. The development of multiplexed biosensors capable of detecting multiple pathogens, including co-infections with Mpox and other viruses, is another promising area. Cross-disciplinary collaborations, especially between materials science, bioengineering, and healthcare technology, are essential for realizing these advancements. This review emphasizes that these advancements hold significant implications for public health by facilitating rapid, accessible Mpox diagnostics, potentially curbing viral spread during outbreaks​.

The practical implications of this research are clear, as nanomaterial-based biosensors can enable more accessible and immediate diagnostics, particularly valuable in remote or resource-limited regions where healthcare infrastructure may be sparse. The theoretical contributions of this review lie in the detailed analysis of nanomaterial interactions with viral components, enriching the understanding of how nanomaterials can be optimized for heightened sensitivity and specificity. Technically, this review provides insights into the diverse mechanisms available for viral detection, setting a framework for further refinement of sensor design to improve robustness, portability, and user-friendliness.

In conclusion, while nanomaterial-based biosensors have not yet reached their full potential in clinical practice, the field is advancing rapidly. Future research should focus on enhancing stability, lowering production costs, and expanding the range of detectable viral biomarkers. Additionally, collaborative initiatives to integrate these sensors into digital health systems can further enhance diagnostic efficiency, contributing to real-time outbreak surveillance and ultimately strengthening global health responses. The progress discussed in this review signifies a significant leap forward, establishing nanomaterial-based biosensors as critical tools in modern virology and public health.
